text: The laparoscopic view shows adhesiolysis in a scarred abdomen, with omental adhesions being carefully freed using cold scissors. A small bleeder is controlled using a bipolar device, ensuring no danger to the bowel during dissection.

The surgeon frees the working area for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, beginning by cutting the lateral attachment of the uterus 1.5 cm outside the uterine corner. The peritoneum is opened between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, creating a small hole under the infundibulopelvic ligament. The peritoneum surrounding the infundibulopelvic ligament is removed to improve hemostasis. A window is created below the infundibulopelvic ligament, reducing the risk of ureteral injury. The peritoneum is cut, allowing safe control of the infundibulopelvic artery and vein using bipolar cautery, with no risk to the aorta.

The surgeon attempts to find the dissecting plane between the bladder and the vagina, with the assistant pulling the uterus and retracting the bladder cranially. The initial step involves removing the peritoneal tissue connecting the bladder and uterus. The dissection continues laterally on the right, mirroring the procedure on the opposite side by opening the peritoneum from the round ligament to the infundibulopelvic ligament.

The surgeon uses gas to assist in dissecting connective tissue within the broad ligament, applying cranial traction to the adnexa. The peritoneum is cut to enhance adnexa mobility, creating a window below the infundibulopelvic ligament to reduce ureteral injury risk. The peritoneal tissue surrounding the infundibulopelvic ligament is cut, allowing control of both uterine artery and vein. The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the uterus, cutting the uterosacral ligament at its uterine insertion. The uterine pedicle on the right is controlled, followed by vesico-uterine mobilization.

The surgeon uses scissors to find the correct plane between the bladder and the vagina, pushing the bladder down on the uterine manipulator. The right bladder pillar is cut medially, clearly revealing the plane between the bladder and vagina. The anterior part of the uterine manipulator is visible, facilitating a safe colpotomy. The surgeon then controls the medial part of the left bladder pillar.

The surgeon controls the bladder pilar, allowing easy movement of the bladder, and cuts through fibrotic tissue. The uterine pedicle on the left is coagulated with a bipolar device over two to three centimeters and then cut. The procedure progresses posteriorly to locate the uterine insertion of the left uterosacral ligament. The uterus is devascularized with its lateral and anterior attachments removed, enabling safe control of the left uterine pedicle. The surgeon prepares to control the cervical vaginal vessel and perform a colpotomy.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to incise the anterior and left lateral edges of the vagina, just medial to the uterine vessel. The needle is placed in the left lateral trocar to complete the posterior part of the colpotomy. The vagina is incised laterally, and the colpotomy is completed, allowing for the vaginal extraction of the small uterus without reduction maneuvers.

The surgeon extracts the uterus through the vaginal canal. Closure of the vaginal edge begins with a 2-0 Vicryl stitch at the lateral part of the vagina. Small bleeders on the vaginal edge are left uncoagulated to promote better healing.

The surgeon begins closing the vaginal cuff using a 2-0 Vicryl suture, starting at the lateral part and proceeding to the medial part. The mucosa is carefully included in the sutures to minimize the risk of postoperative vaginal bleeding. The intracorporeal knotting technique is used, resulting in a complete and secure closure of the vagina.
